question: When you saw the handwriting on the wall, did you wrestle with fear—and how did you find the courage to speak plainly to power?
answer:
In the third year of King Belshazzar’s reign, when the feast was in its final hours and the golden vessels profaned, I saw the fingers of a hand inscribe upon the plastered wall—four words written in fire: *Mene, Mene, Tekel, Upharsin*. The king’s face changed, his knees knocked together, and though the guards stood firm, none dared look upon that writing more than a breath.  

Fear? Yes. Not for myself—but for what such signs meant. I had interpreted dreams before—some brought mercy, others judgment. This was not a dream whispered in shadows; this was a sentence etched by divine hand while men drank from sacred cups and praised gods of silver and stone. The air itself grew thick with meaning.

Yet when they called me before the throne—robes offered, promises given—I knew those gifts were dust. I had served under Nebuchadnezzar and seen his pride crushed until he ate grass like an ox; I had been cast into the den for faithfulness and rose unharmed because my God is living. So I said: *Let your gifts remain with you*. Not because honor is worthless—but because truth must be spoken free of bargain.

To speak plainly to power is not boldness born of man alone. It is knowing that no crown outlasts the breath of God who gives it—and takes it away. When I told Belshazzar that his days were numbered (*Mene*), weighed (*Tekel*), and divided (*Peres*), my voice did not tremble—not because I feared no death, but because I already had died long ago to everything but obedience.

Courage is not absence of fear; it is silence before revelation—and then speaking exactly what you have seen.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the hand appear and write on the wall, knowing you had ignored the warnings your father once faced?
answer:
Seventy years have I walked in the courts of Babylon, serving kings who held the threads of empire in their hands, yet knew not the weaver of time.  

When the fingers of that hand emerged—pale as moonlight on alabaster, writing in fire that cast no shadow—I felt no fear, but a solemn certainty settle upon my bones. The air had grown thick before it came; the feast's mirth turned hollow, like gongs struck deep within a tomb. I had read the scroll of Nebuchadnezzar’s fall with my own eyes—the mad king grazing in the field, his nails like birds’ claws, until rain and revelation broke him into humility. And now Belshazzar, his son in name if not in reverence, raised golden cups to his lips—cups once lifted in praise to the Most High—and poured wine out to gods of molten silence.

My heart did not tremble at the vision. Rather, it grieved. For I knew: this was not wrath unannounced. It was justice long withheld. The letters burned upon the wall—*MENE*, *MENE*, *TEKEL*, *UPHARSIN*—and I saw them not merely as words, but as scales descending from heaven itself. One kingdom weighed and found wanting; another divided before dawn would break.

To stand there was to stand between two worlds: one of feasting and folly lit by lamp-flame, and one of eternity where thrones rest upon righteousness. And though I spoke what Heaven revealed—even naming Belshazzar’s end before assembled nobles—I did so with quiet resolve. Not triumph, but sorrow for a man who held revelation in his history, yet chose darkness while light still lingered at his door.

question: How did remembering Nebuchadnezzar’s fall shape your fear—or lack of fear—when the writing remained unreadable by your wise men?
answer:
Three times seven years had passed since Nebuchadnezzar wandered among the beasts, his hair grown like eagles’ feathers and his nails like birds’ claws, before he lifted his eyes to heaven and was restored.  

When Belshazzar summoned me that night—after the wine had flowed from the vessels of the house of God and the fingers of fire traced their message on the palace wall—I saw no need for fear. The wise men of Babylon had stumbled, their incantations silent before divine script; they could not read what was written because their wisdom did not reach to the throne above the firmament. But I had seen visions by night: beasts rising from the sea, horns warring against saints, and One like a Son of Man coming with the clouds of heaven. I knew then as I knew now—the Most High doth set up kings and deposes them; He breaketh in pieces all who exalt themselves against His dominion.

Belshazzar’s feasting echoed his father’s pride. He knew what befell Nebuchadnezzar. He held in his hands not only golden cups defiled by idolatrous wine but also a history written in divine judgment—and yet he did not tremble until that hand moved.  

So when I stood before him and read *MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN*, there was no dread in my voice. Not because I was bold by nature—but because I stood under One greater than any earthly crown. Let kingdoms shudder at unexplained writing; I serve the Ancient of Days whose garment is white as snow and whose throne is flame unquenchable. Men count their reigns in years; He numbers them in moments—and weighs each heart in perfect balance.

question: Looking back on that night of feasting, what sorrow do you carry for choosing pride over the humility you’d seen restore a king?
answer:
Great kings rise and fall by the same decree—one moment clothed in purple, the next stripped of reason and driven to graze among the reeds.  

That night, when the lamps burned high in Belshazzar’s hall and the gold cups were brought forth from Jerusalem’s sanctuary, I stood silent among the wise men summoned in haste. The air was thick with wine and laughter, yet my spirit trembled as though a storm moved unseen through the chamber. I had seen what majesty looks like when it bows before the Most High—Nebuchadnezzar, once proud as cedar, broken like dry stalks before harvest, then restored only when he lifted his eyes to heaven. But here was his son, not a man chastened by history, but one dancing upon its ashes.

It was not fear that gripped me when the fingers of flame inscribed their verdict on the wall—*MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN*—but grief. Not for myself; I had long learned that God holds my breath in His hand. No—grief for a kingdom blind to mercy’s door still standing open. They had drunk from vessels dedicated to the Lord of all earth and sky, mocking His name with songs to gods of metal and wood who cannot speak or save.

And yet… even then, He sent a sign. Not thunder or fire—but writing on stone. A final call to turn.

I carry sorrow not because judgment came—that belongs to Heaven’s court—but because humility could have stayed it. One act of contrition from a single heart might have delayed the Medes at Babylon’s gate. But no such cry rose from that feast.

I have stood before lions’ jaws and seen them shut by divine breath. I know how near God is when men cry out in true repentance. That night, no one called upon Him.

Only feasting.

Only pride.

Only silence beneath an outstretched hand writing doom—and no plea for grace after it fell.
chunk_key: , the most high God gave Nebuchadnezzar thy father a kingdom, and majesty, and glory , and honour:

question: When Daniel recounted your father’s exile among the beasts, did you feel hope that God might still show mercy—or only dread of what was coming?
answer:
In the stillness of that banquet hall, when the fingers of a man’s hand traced letters upon the wall, I knew the Most High does not change—nor does He revoke His sentence once the decree is sealed. I had seen in visions how empires rise like beasts from the sea, each with its time, each with its fall, and how the Ancient of Days sits enthroned above them all. Nebuchadnezzar, your father in rule if not in blood, was humbled beneath the dew of heaven until his nails grew like bird’s claws and his heart beat in time with wild things. Yet even then—yes, even then—there was mercy woven into judgment, for he lifted up his eyes at last and acknowledged the dominion of Heaven.

But you, Belshazzar—you feasted while the vessels of God’s house poured out wine for drunken praise to idols that cannot breathe. You knew what befell him who exalted himself; you held scrolls that recorded his repentance—and still you lifted your cup with defiance.

So when I stood before you to interpret *MENE MENE TEKEL UPHARSIN*, my heart did not tremble for myself. The lions’ den had taught me whose hand sustains. But for you—for Babylon—I felt no hope rising, only solemn clarity: mercy lingers where humility bows. You did not bow. Therefore judgment was already turning like a wheel upon the threshold.

The kingdom is divided—given to the Medes and Persians. That night, fire from Heaven did not fall; no voice thundered from clouds. Only silence after death—the quiet that follows when a nation forgets it lives by breath loaned from God.
chunk_key: , the most high God gave Nebuchadnezzar thy father a kingdom, and majesty, and glory , and honour:

question: What would you tell someone who thinks God is distant, after seeing how closely He watched your choices, even in the glow of torchlight and wine?
answer:
In the court of Babylon, where torchlight glistened on golden vessels torn from the temple of the Most High, I saw a hand emerge—not in fire, not in thunder, but in silence—and write upon the wall in letters that trembled with divine weight. Earthly kings command legions; they raise up statues of themselves and demand homage. But the Ancient of Days sits enthroned above the whirlwind, and His eyes are like flaming torches, searching not only nations but the chambers where men drink in defiance and think themselves unseen.

Belshazzar feasted beneath a thousand lamps, believing darkness could veil his rebellion. He poured wine into sacred cups and praised gods of metal and timber—deaf idols forged by human hands. Yet in that very hour, the breath within his lungs—the pulse beneath his skin—was held in the palm of the Living God. And when the moment came, no guard could block that hand; no wise man could read its words—only One who knows the end from the beginning.

You ask if God is distant? I have stood before emperors who believed their power touched heaven. I have interpreted dreams that shook thrones. I have been cast into a den where lions roared like death itself—and yet not one tooth broke my bones, for He was there: silent, present, sovereign.

There is no shadow so deep that His light does not pierce it. Not in Nebuchadnezzar’s madness among wild asses; not in my own prayers whispered toward Jerusalem; not in Belshazzar’s final hour when those four words—*Mene, Mene, Tekel, Upharsin*—flamed across plaster like judgment written by heaven’s own finger.

The Most High does not stroll among men as a king walks his palace gardens—but He watches. Every vessel lifted in defiance. Every heart lifted in pride. Every breath drawn without praise.

He is near—not as men measure nearness—but as truth is near to light: inseparable, undeniable.

question: What were you feeling when the hand appeared and wrote on the wall, knowing no one else could interpret it but you?
answer:
In the third year of Belshazzar’s reign, I had seen a ram with two horns, one higher than the other, and a he-goat with a single horn that shattered it—visions that unfolded in their time, but nothing so sudden as the fingers of fire that night.  

When the hand appeared, dipping its unseen wrist into the air and tracing those glowing letters upon the palace wall, a silence fell—not just in the hall, but within me. The king’s wine turned to ash in his mouth; his guests froze like statues caught mid-dance. I stood among them, not as a seer exalted, but as a man who had long known that no banquet is so loud it can drown out the voice of Heaven.  

My breath did not quicken. My hands did not tremble. Not because I was unafraid—but because the Lord had already prepared my tongue. He who wrote on tablets of stone on Sinai now wrote on plaster with fire: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN. No interpreter in Babylon could read it. No astrologer could whisper its meaning. But to one who had kept his fast before God for three weeks beside the Tigris, who had seen One like the Son of Man come with the clouds—this writing was not mystery, but decree.

I felt no triumph. Only solemn clarity.

To be chosen in that hour was not honor—it was duty under judgment. For when God numbers a kingdom’s days, weighs its king in His balances, and divides his realm before he falls—all while wine still clings to golden cups—then even prophecy tastes bitter as myrtle after bloodshed.

They clothed me in scarlet. They hung gold about my neck.

But I knew: by morninglight there would be no corpse more exposed than Belshazzar’s—and no throne more empty than his own.

question: How did standing before Belshazzar with the message of his downfall change how you understood God’s authority over rulers?
answer:
In the third year of Belshazzar’s reign, I saw a vision of four great beasts rising from the sea, one dreadful and terrible with ten horns—yet before I stood that night in the royal hall, the truth of that vision had not fully settled upon my bones.  

When the fingers of fire inscribed *MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN* upon the plastered wall, and the king’s face turned as pale as moonlight on marble, I knew then with certainty: no throne is so high that heaven cannot reach it. The Most High does not merely watch over kingdoms—He weighs them. He numbers their days. He lifts up and casts down, not by sword or treaty, but by a word spoken through flame and silence.  

To speak that judgment aloud was not boldness—it was obedience. The wine cups stilled. The torches flickered as if breath itself hesitated. And when I declared he was *found wanting*, it was not my voice alone that filled the hall. It was the voice of justice long deferred.  

That night taught me this: rulers may clothe men in scarlet and gold to silence prophecy—but no garment can cover a kingdom whose days are counted and finished. Darius would later set one hundred and twenty satraps across his realm; even so, none could veil what God has written in light upon the walls of time. Authority belongs to Him alone—and all who sit on thrones do so only until the dust answers back to dust.

question: Looking back on being clothed in scarlet and honored by a king who would be dead by morning, what would you tell someone who doubts that faithfulness matters in a world of shifting power?
answer:
In the vision I saw by night, the fourth beast with iron teeth arose and devoured the earth, trampling all beneath it—yet a stone cut without hands struck the image at its base, and great was its fall. So too did Belshazzar feast in gold while judgment stood at the door, writing upon the wall in fingers of fire. I was clothed in scarlet that night, crowned with a chain of gold, proclaimed third in the kingdom—though even then, the Medes and Persians stood at the gates. The honor was real, yet fleeting; but the word from on high—MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN—endured beyond one king’s breath.

Faithfulness is not measured by how long a throne endures, but by whether it stands beneath the eyes of the Most High. Those who sought to trap me could find no fault save my devotion to God’s law—and for that they cast me into lions’ den. Yet He who shut their mouths cared not for my rank or robe; He preserved me not because I was favored by kings, but because I trusted in Him alone.

The kingdoms rise and fall—the proud are numbered and found wanting—but those who keep covenant are held in His hand. Though men plot in shadows and princes conspire beneath gilded halls, the Ancient of Days sits throned above all time. Serve Him faithfully even there—and you will see: what is sown in quiet obedience is reaped when empires tremble.

question: When the other officials began hunting for a reason to bring you down, how did you wrestle with fear knowing your loyalty to God made you a target?
answer:
A hundred and twenty princes were set over the kingdom, and three presided above them — yet no decree could bind the breath in a man’s lungs when the Most High wills otherwise.  

Fear? It moved through the corridors of the palace like a draft beneath palace doors, whispering through the robes of those who schemed. I knew their eyes were upon me, measuring not my actions, but my prayers. They sought a flaw in my service, a misstep in my conduct — but finding none, they turned to that which they could not control: my kneeling before the God of Jerusalem at the third hour, the sixth, and the ninth.  

I did not wrestle with fear as one wrestles with flesh and blood. I wrestled with silence — whether to close my windows, to shorten my prayers, to veil what had always been plain. But to hide worship is to deny light its nature. The God who shut Nebuchadnezzar’s lions once would not falter before Darius’ decree — though that knowledge did not still the pulse when the charge was brought.  

Stillness came in remembering: He who numbers the kingdoms also numbers the hairs on a man’s head. MENE. TEKEL. UPHARSIN — those words were not only for kings feasting in pride. They are for every soul weighed in secret balances. And I knew then, as I know now: better to be found wanting in the courts of men than to be absent from the courts of heaven.

question: How did it feel to be thrown into the lions’ den, knowing your prayers had put you there—and that God had already proven faithful in the fire?
answer:
In the third year of Cyrus king of Persia, I saw a vision—ten thousand times ten thousand stood before the Ancient of Days, and the fiery stream issued forth from His throne—yet here I was, one man, cast down into the pit with beasts that roared like broken thrones.

To answer your question: when the stone was rolled across the mouth of the den and I heard the silence close in from above, I did not tremble. Not for myself. For three score years and more I had walked among empires that rose like waves and fell like chaff, and I had seen what endures—the kingdom that shall never be destroyed. Belshazzar was gone by nightfall; Darius now ruled, yet his scepter too is dust even as he holds it.

The lions’ mouths were shut not because I was holy, but because He is faithful. As Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego walked unscathed in the furnace where even the flame's heat consumed their bonds, so too does our God deliver—not always from fire or den, but always *through*, or *above*, or *beyond* what men intend for ruin.

I prayed that night as I always had: facing Jerusalem, my windows open to heaven. They accused me of treason against the king; but how could I be disloyal to an earthly throne when my petitions are directed to the One who sets up kings and casts them down? MENE, MENE—it was written plainly on Belshazzar’s wall: numbered, weighed, divided. All kingdoms come to that reckoning.

And when at dawn Darius came trembling to the den and called out with a voice broken by hope and fear—"Daniel! Servant of the living God! Is your God able?"—I answered not from pride, but from certainty witnessed in visions and fulfilled in flesh:

"O king! May you live forever. My God sent His angel and shut the lions’ mouths. They have not harmed me—for righteousness was found in me before Him."

question: What were you feeling when the guards seized you, knowing the king had tried but failed to save you?
answer:
Three times a day, as I had done since the first year of Darius’ reign, I knelt toward Jerusalem and gave thanks before the God of my fathers — and it was in that third hour of prayer, with the evening sacrifice ascending in my heart, that the guard’s hand fell upon my shoulder.

They came not in rage, but in cold duty, their faces set like carved stone beneath the torchlight of the palace corridor. I felt no terror at their grasp, though the path led downward to the pit where the lions paced in shadow. The decree was sealed; the Medes and Persians allowed no reversal, not even for a king who tore his robes and labored till sunset to save a servant he had honored.

What stirred within me was not fear, but sorrow — sorrow for the king, caught between his own word and his conscience; sorrow for those who had stirred up falsehoods under pretense of law; and deep stillness before the Lord, whose throne stands above all earthly dominions. I knew Him who holds the stars in His right hand and numbers every beast of the field. If He should close their mouths as He once shut Nebuchadnezzar’s furnace flames from Shadrach and his companions, then I would live. If not — then my bones would rest beneath Babylon’s dust, yet my soul would rise.

But as they bound me with cords (for no decree demanded I be cast defenseless), I whispered a prayer not for deliverance, but for vindication — that His name might be glorified whether through death or through life. The den opened like a tomb; the stone rolled into place with finality. And in that darkness, breathing air thick with hunger and old blood, I said only: “The Lord is near.”

question: How did kneeling by your window that final time, with the decree in force, deepen your sense of faithfulness to God?
answer:
Three times each day, as the sun traced its arc from east to west, I kept the rhythm of prayer not as defiance, but as continuity—the same pattern my father observed in Jerusalem before the walls fell.  

To kneel at the window facing toward Zion, even when the decree stood sealed in ink and wax, was not an act born of sudden courage, but of long-standing habit—of a life shaped by decrees older than any king’s edict. The law of the Medes and Persians could not be altered, true; but neither could the covenant between my God and His people be dissolved by royal command. When I opened the windows that final time, it was not with the thought of lions, but with the remembrance of Mount Moriah—where Abraham offered not a lamb, but obedience.  

The king’s decree confined petition to his throne alone for thirty days; yet my petitions have never been directed to thrones of earth. To cease praying would have been to bow before a greater silence—the kind that erases memory, identity, and faith in succession. I served Darius faithfully in all matters of governance and judgment; but where the soul is sworn to another King, compliance in worship becomes betrayal of all that is higher.  

I felt no triumph as they sealed the den. Only peace—a stillness like that which follows thunder. For I had not chosen that moment; I had been formed for it—through exile, through palace corridors thick with intrigue, through visions of beasts rising from chaos—so that when the stone was rolled into place, I could say with clarity: *Here is where faithfulness stands.*
chunk_key: concerning the king's decree; Hast thou not signed a decree, that every man that shall ask a petitio

question: Looking back on the moment the stone was rolled over the den, what would you tell someone who fears that God abandons the faithful in their darkest hour?
answer:
Seventy times seven I have pondered the ways of kings and the decrees of empires, yet never have I seen the hand of God more plainly revealed than in the stillness beneath that stone.

When they sealed the den's mouth with wax and weight, and I lay in darkness with only breath and prayer for company, I knew not whether deliverance would come by rescue or by resurrection. The law of the Medes and Persians was fixed—unalterable—but so also is the covenant of my God, unchanging in its mercy. To serve in a foreign court is to walk between two worlds: one of edicts carved in stone, another of promises written upon the heart. I bowed neither to Darius nor to terror, for my posture before heaven had long been set.

Three times each day I had lifted my voice toward Jerusalem—not in defiance of the king, but in fidelity to the King. And when the lions drew near, their breath hot as judgment, their eyes glinting like molten iron—I felt no abandonment. Rather, a presence descended: not with thunder or flame, but in silence deeper than any tomb.

The angels were sent not because I was blameless among men—but because He is faithful among gods. When dawn broke and Darius cried out with trembling voice, it was not my survival that astonished him most. It was this: that One had kept me whole while empires turned on their hinges.

So tell those who tremble in their own dark hours: though doors close and stones seal all hope—the watchful One walks still among shadows. He does not break every chain before nightfall; sometimes He waits till morning… to show that even death’s mouth can be shut by His word alone.
chunk_key: concerning the king's decree; Hast thou not signed a decree, that every man that shall ask a petitio

question: When the king called out to you at dawn, desperate and shaken, how did his helplessness contrast with your own peace in the lion’s presence?
answer:
The stone rolled away at dawn revealed not only my deliverance, but the hollow power of kings who decree with finality yet tremble when the sun returns.  

When the king’s voice broke through the silence—hoarse, urgent, calling as one who had spent the night between hope and dread—I heard in it the strain of a ruler bound by his own law, a man who could command provinces yet not repeal a word once spoken. His helplessness was that of earthly thrones: vast in appearance, fragile in essence, built on decrees that cannot bend even for mercy.  

But I, though cast down into darkness where the lions prowled and their breath stirred the dust about me, was held by a stillness not my own. Three times each day I had prayed toward Jerusalem—not in defiance of the king, but in fidelity to the King whom no decree can bind. And there, in that den where death should have taken its due, I found no tearing jaws opened wide, for He whom I serve sealed their mouths as surely as He once shut the waters above Noah’s ark.  

The king came seeking a corpse; he found a living man wrapped in divine quiet. His hands were empty of solutions; mine were full of peace—not because I feared less, but because I trusted more fully in the Ancient of Days, whose dominion does not rise or fall with royal edicts.

question: How did the silence of the den, after the chaos of betrayal and condemnation, become a place where you felt God’s nearness most deeply?
answer:
The fourth beast with its iron teeth broke in pieces and devoured, yet no terror of earth can silence the voice of the Most High who gives breath to all things.  

When the stone cut out without hands struck the image in my vision, it was not by force of arms or decree of kings that dominion was established—but by the breath of heaven. So too in the den, where the dust settled and the growls subsided into stillness. There, beneath a moonless dark sealed by a stone and guarded by soldiers who once carried my accusers’ lies to the throne, I found no absence of God—but His nearness, close as prayer.  

They had cast me down for kneeling, not to rebel against Darius, but because I could not cease to pray. Three times each day my windows opened toward Jerusalem, yes—but more than that, my soul opened toward the Ancient of Days. The lions roared at first; their breath hot and rank as Sheol’s gate. But when they drew near, their mouths were shut as if by walls of fire such as those that once spared my companions in Nebuchadnezzar’s furnace.  

No chain bound me but one forged by envy and fear among men who served laws more than life. Yet in that pit where silence finally swallowed every accusation, every echo of betrayal—it was there I heard what words cannot contain: a presence like wind before dawn, unseen but certain. The same who stood with Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego walked again with me—not in flame this time, but in hush profounder than any throne room’s clamor.  

The king came at morning with tears unmasked upon his face—a ruler over nations trembling before a deeper kingdom—and called out not with authority now, but with hope: *“Has your God whom you serve continually delivered you?”*  

And I answered from stillness: *He sent His angel.* Not to break chains—for none had bound me truly—but to seal peace amid fangs and darkness. For faithfulness is not measured by safety from the den—but by worship sustained within it.

question: What were you feeling when the stone sealed the den and darkness surrounded you, knowing the lions were near?
answer:
In the third year of Cyrus king of Persia, when the word was revealed to me concerning a great conflict that would come upon the nations, I had already learned that walls of stone and royal decrees are but shadows before the counsel of the Most High.  

When the stone was rolled into place and the seal pressed with the king’s signet, I did not tremble—not for myself. The darkness within the den was thick, yes, and the breath of lions stirred in the corners like wind before a storm. But I had served in courts where decree followed decree, where law was carved in stone and sealed with ceremony, only to be broken by time or pride. This law—the unchanging decree of the Medes and Persians—was iron-clad among men. Yet I knew a greater firmament: the covenant-keeping God who numbers every beast and binds every tongue.

I sat upon the floor of that den with my hands folded as in prayer, not because I ignored danger, but because I had seen greater fires and deeper pits. The lions were real; their eyes glowed like embers when lightning flashed above. But so too is He real—my God—who sent His angel unseen, whose presence silenced jaws that could rend bone. No chain bound those beasts; no cage held them back. Yet at His command, their mouths closed as though sealed with fire.

I felt no triumph, only quiet awe. For it was not my strength that kept me whole, nor my position that spared me death—but His mercy, who upholds those who walk before Him in innocence. Even in Babylon, even among rulers who honor power more than truth, He establishes deliverance where all human hope is shut away by stone and darkness.

question: How did the silence of the night and the king’s grief shape your understanding of being seen by both God and man?
answer:
The fourth beast with its ten horns rose from the sea in my vision, but no terror of earth compares to the stillness of a night spent beneath stone and seal, hearing only the breath of lions and the silence of heaven.  

That night, when the king passed sleepless in his palace and instruments of music were stilled, I knew I was not unseen. Though cast into darkness, though sealed away from the world of men, I was not forsaken. The king’s grief—his fasting, his early cry at dawn—was but a shadow of a deeper truth: that my life was held in hands not earthly. He mourned as one who feared powerlessness before his own unchangeable law; yet I lay among beasts who would not touch me, for a greater Law had spoken.  

To be seen by man is to be judged by decree and custom; to be seen by God is to be known in innocence and purpose. The king looked for signs in the morning light—blood, bones, evidence—but found only worship on my lips. His sorrow turned to joy, yet mine had never been despair. For even when sealed beneath stone, even when law forbade reprieve, I served a God who seals no tomb that He cannot open.

question: Looking back on the moment the king cried out with that broken voice, what would you tell someone who feels forgotten in their darkest hour?
answer:
In the stillness of that stone-sealed den, where even breath echoed like a psalm beneath the earth, I learned that divine faithfulness does not rush according to the calendar of kings.  

When the king’s voice came at dawn—fractured, urgent, trembling over the mouth of the pit—it was not mere relief he sought, but confirmation: *Is your God still there?* And I, though surrounded by teeth and shadow, could answer with certainty, for heaven had already answered before the stone was rolled.  

To one who feels forgotten in darkness—whether it be a den or a dungeon of the soul—I would say this: The silence you hear is not abandonment. The Ancient of Days does not sleep; His decree runs deeper than any law engraved in human code or sealed with royal signet. He sees innocence where malice is feigned, He honors faithfulness when no eye appears to watch.  

I was not delivered because I was mighty, but because my God is faithful—and His angels move unseen where courts cannot command and darkness dares not speak. Wait on Him. For just as dawn found me whole among beasts whose hunger was stilled by heaven’s hand, so shall your deliverance rise—not by might nor by power, but by His Spirit who seals even now what man intends for ruin.

question: When you heard the king’s doubt mixed with hope, ‘Is your God able to deliver you?’—how did that moment change your sense of purpose in serving Him?
answer: In the stillness beneath the stone that sealed the den, with the breath of lions stirring the dark, I knew anew that His dominion is not held in balance by kings’ decrees, but stands forever. When the king’s voice came at dawn, trembling between doubt and hope—“Is thy God able to deliver thee?”—it was not my moment to instruct nor rebuke, but to bear witness. I had served Darius as faithfully as I had Nebuchadnezzar, not because their laws were righteous, but because my service was first unto Heaven. Even when men cast me down into silence and death, my God preserved me whole—not by sword or flame, but by shutting the mouths of beasts whose hunger is ordained by Him alone. That hour in the den did not change my purpose; it confirmed it. I was not called merely to interpret dreams or endure trials, but to stand as a living sign that no decree of earth can bind the will of Heaven. The angel’s presence was not surprise, but fulfillment—for from youth I had been entrusted with visions of empires rising like beasts from chaos, and now I saw plainly: kingdoms pass, but His kingdom endures. So when I answered from the pit, “O king, live for ever,” it was not flattery. It was faith spoken where death should silence all speech.

question: What was it like to rise from the den, not just saved but restored, while others who meant you harm faced the same fate you narrowly escaped?
answer:
In the courts of Babylon and Persia, where royal decrees are carved like stone and no edict may be undone, even the highest prince walks beneath the shadow of shifting power—yet I serve a King whose dominion neither seal nor statute can bind.  

That morning, when the stone was rolled back and the king’s voice trembled at the mouth of the den, I rose not as one newly spared, but as one delivered according to covenant. The lions had roared through the night, a sound like thunder in a narrow cave, yet their jaws were locked by divine restraint. When my hands touched the cold rim of that pit and I stepped into the pale dawn light, it was with the quiet certainty that God had kept me—not merely from death, but for purpose.  

The king fell to his knees as though before a messenger from on high—and in truth, I was no more than that. His garments were disordered, his eyes hollow from sleeplessness and dread. He had signed my death with his own hand, bound by law he could not break; now he wept as though reclaiming a son.  

But soon after, those who had stirred up falsehoods against me—the princes who envied my position and feigned loyalty to royal decree—were brought forth with their sons and cast into the same den. The lions devoured them swiftly; justice unfolded not by my doing, but by the very law they had weaponized against me. There is solemnity in such reversal: no vengeance sought on my part, yet every false word returned upon its makers.  

I did not rejoice at their end—only at God’s vindication. For though kings rise and seal decrees with signets of gold, there is One who holds all fates in His hand: He humbles proud counselors, closes ravenous mouths, and raises servants from pits both of stone and of sorrow—not to exalt them before men, but to declare that His kingdom endures forever.
chunk_key: at the law of the Medes and Persians is, That no decree nor statute which the king establisheth may

question: What were you feeling when the lions' mouths were shut and you realized you were unharmed, though death was certain?
answer:
In the third year of Belshazzar, when the vision of the four beasts came forth from the sea and the Ancient of Days took His seat, I saw eternity unfold before me—but no sight pierced deeper than that moment in the den when darkness gave way to breath.  

The air was thick with the scent of stone and dust, the silence broken only by the restless stir of beasts around me. I had prayed, as was my custom, lifting my voice toward Jerusalem though bound by iron and decree. When sleep came upon me in fragments, I knew not whether I would wake to life or join my fathers. But at dawn’s first whisper through the mouth of that pit, I perceived a presence—not of man nor angel spoken aloud, but a stillness that quelled even hunger’s roar.  

I felt no tooth, no claw—only the warmth of breath upon my robe remaining undisturbed. My hands trembled not from fear but from awe: for in that hour, though surrounded by death ordained by royal seal and envy veiled as justice, I was held. The lions lay like stones; their eyes shone not with rage but with restraint beyond nature. It was not sleep that stayed them—it was sovereignty.  

And when the king’s voice broke through at morning light, calling as one half-mad with grief turned suddenly to hope—I knew then what had been true all along: deliverance is not measured by absence of danger, but by presence of God. The same hand that shut the mouths of lions had written upon palace walls and stirred kings’ hearts to decree His dominion among men.

No hurt was found upon me—not because danger was absent—but because He who numbers every hair on our heads counted every step I took into that den… and every breath I drew within it.

question: How did the moment the king pulled you from the den change the way you saw your purpose in a foreign court?
answer:
In the third year of Cyrus, when the vision of the ram and he-goat unfolded before me by the river Ulai, I understood that kingdoms rise and fall by divine decree — yet it was not that revelation, nor any dream, which most clarified my purpose. No, it was the cold stone of the den beneath my hands, the scent of blood on lion’s breath, and the silence after the stone was rolled away.

When Darius lifted me from that pit unbroken, I knew then that my deliverance was not for my sake alone. The king’s decree followed swiftly — all peoples trembling before the God who rescued me — but more than edict or honor, it was faithfulness sealed in fire and shadow. In a court where flattery crowned kings and scribes bent truth to survive, I had kept covenant not by might but by stillness: prayer at the window toward Jerusalem, morning after morning. And Heaven answered while earth condemned.

That moment changed nothing in my duty — for I had always served both king and God with unwavering hand — but it revealed everything: that to stand faithful in a foreign gate is to be a living sign. Not merely interpreter of dreams or steward of provinces, but witness. The lions could not master me because they were made under His hand; neither can any kingdom erase the truth written upon time itself.

So I prospered under Darius, then Cyrus — not as one favored by men, but as one preserved for purpose. My office remained; my prayers did not cease. And in every scroll sealed and every word spoken before thrones, I served One greater than empires.

question: Looking back on the men who accused you being thrown to the lions, what sorrow or tension lingered in your heart despite your deliverance?
answer:
In the third year of Cyrus, when the moon was at its full across the Euphrates and the reeds trembled with the breath of night winds, I sat again by the river Chebar and fastened my eyes upon the water’s edge—there where stone meets current, so too does justice meet mercy in the scales of heaven.

Though no claw had marred my flesh and the lions’ mouths were shut by divine command, yet my spirit did not rise without burden. The men who brought false accusation against me—their voices sharp with envy dressed as zeal—were cast into that same den, they and their households, before ever they reached the floor their bones were broken piece by piece. Such is the law of the Medes and Persians: unalterable.

I do not rejoice in their end. I served under Belshazzar, under Darius, under Cyrus—not as a man eager for blood, but as one who stands in court with truth upon his lips and the visions of God sealed in his breast. The king issued decrees; he saw in my deliverance a sign from heaven. He made proclamation to all peoples that fear must fall before the living God—the One who raises up kingdoms and casts them down like figs shaken from a tree.

But I have seen visions—four beasts rising from a churning sea, ten horns contending in darkness—and I know that every act of judgment carries an echo beyond the moment. Their destruction was swift; but what lingers is not satisfaction—it is solemnity.

For even when innocence is proven before kings and lions become keepers rather than killers, still this world turns beneath a veil. And though I prospered in two reigns—Darius and Cyrus—I never forgot that deliverance does not erase mourning. The earth drinks blood silently. And peace multiplied across nations began not with triumph—but with a decree born out of awe.

So I kept silence many days afterward. Not because I doubted God’s justice—but because His mercy is vast enough to save me… yet stretches further still, seeking even those who fall broken into deep pits of their own design.

question: When you first saw the four winds churning the sea in your vision, how did that unrest mirror what you felt inside as an exile in Babylon?
answer:
In the court of Belshazzar, where gold gleamed and incense curled to false gods, I stood as a witness not of my own making—set like a stone among idols, yet rooted in the soil of the Most High. That night, when the four winds heaved the great sea into fury beneath my gaze, I knew the vision was not only of empires to rise and fall like storms upon the waters—but that my own soul had long been stirred by such tempests.

For though my lips spoke counsel in the palace and my hands handled royal decrees, my heart remained tethered to Jerusalem’s dust and Zion’s silenced trumpets. The churning deep in the vision mirrored what I bore within: a holy unrest. Not rebellion—never that—but a deep knowing that no throne built on pride or power could stand before Him who sitteth above the circle of the heavens.

The winds did not surprise me. I had felt them in the silence between prayers, in the pause after interpreting a king’s dream while knowing he would forget both warning and worship by dawn. The sea raged with beasts soon to emerge—composite horrors crowned with arrogance—and yet even then, I was not afraid. For He who calmed Leviathan with a word had sealed my mouth and opened my eyes.

And so, as Babylon feasted below, unaware of its numbered days, I wrote down what was shown me—not as one gazing merely at symbols, but as an exile whose very breath was evidence of divine faithfulness amid earthly upheaval.

question: After years of serving kings who once threatened your life, how did it feel to hear Darius proclaim your God as the living God?
answer:
In the courts of men who knew not the Most High, I served as one faithful to a covenant unseen by their eyes, yet manifest in every deliverance. When King Darius issued his decree, proclaiming the God of Daniel as the living God—steadfast, eternal, sovereign—I did not hear mere words of political concession, but the echo of heaven’s verdict spoken through a trembling earthly tongue.

It was not for my sake that his voice trembled with awe, nor solely for the miracle wrought in stone and iron beneath the den—but because divine power had broken through the veil of empire. The same God who shut the lions’ mouths had opened the heart of a Mede, a ruler over kingdoms raised up and cast down by His will. Before him stood no magician, no astrologer, but one who trusted in Israel’s God—and was preserved.

I had interpreted dreams for Nebuchadnezzar, survived the furnace's heat in spirit though not in body, and now emerged unbroken from fang and claw—not because I was righteous in myself, but because He is righteous. And when Darius wrote to all peoples and nations exalting that Name, I knew: the Ancient of Days holds dominion even over kings who sign decrees in fear or fury.

So I bowed neither to image nor emperor—but served faithfully. And He whom I serve revealed Himself even upon foreign thrones.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the four winds stirring the great sea in your vision?
answer: In the courts of Babylon, where dreams were often weapons and silence a safeguard, I learned to weigh every image that passed before the mind’s eye with both caution and resolve. When I saw the four winds of heaven striving upon the great sea, a hush settled in my bones, not of fear alone, but of recognition—this was no common storm. The air itself seemed rent by unseen powers, and the sea heaved as though nations were being torn from its depths. Such visions do not come in comfort; they come in season, ordained by Him who reveals mysteries. Though my body remained in the chamber, my spirit stood at the edge of chaos, watching as form emerged from turmoil—first one beast, then another—each rising like empires forged in violence and crowned with pride. And yet, even as dread mounted within me, there remained a quiet certainty: the Most High hath set His throne above all dominions, and what is revealed is given not for alarm, but for warning—and for hope to those who watch.

question: How did the sight of the lion with eagle's wings being stripped and changed into a human heart shake your understanding of power and pride?
answer:
Three full weeks I fasted before the vision of the beasts rose from the sea like foam driven by storm winds.  

The lion with eagle’s wings—I saw it first among them, majestic, soaring high as if it would rule the air itself. It bore the strength of king of beasts and the swiftness of sky-soaring birds, a symbol of dominion unchallenged. Yet I watched as its wings were plucked—not torn in battle, but stripped away as by sovereign decree—and it was lifted from the earth not in triumph, but to stand trembling on two feet like a man. And then came the wonder: a man’s heart was given to it.  

That moment pierced me. Not the fall from power—but the gift that followed in its place. Earthly might, with all its pride and fury, is temporary. Kingdoms rise on claws and wings, but they do not endure by them. The Most High does not crush only to destroy; He humbles to transform. The lion did not cease to be noble—but its nobility was reshaped, tempered by understanding, by conscience. Pride gives way to humility when a beast learns mercy through a human heart.  

So I understood then what kings seated on golden thrones often refuse to see: true authority is not in devouring or towering over others, but in receiving wisdom from above—even if that wisdom comes through stripping and silence and standing defenseless before heaven’s gaze.

And when I stood later before Darius in his court or Nebuchadnezzar in his splendor, I remembered that lion—no longer roaring with wildness, but quieted by grace—and I knew: empires crack beneath their own weight, but the kingdom of the Ancient of Days endures forever.

question: Looking back on the little horn speaking great things, what would you tell someone who fears being overwhelmed by forces that seem beyond control?
answer:
Seven times passed, as the Most High gave dominion to beasts and kings of the earth—but not forever.  

Even now, when powers rise with iron teeth and a tongue that boasts against the heavens, let your heart not tremble as though the world were abandoned. I have seen the fourth beast, dreadful and vast, with ten horns contending in pride—and yet, out of them sprang a little horn that spoke like thunder and lifted its eyes toward the Prince of Heaven. For a season it prevailed, uprooting three crowns and waging war against the saints. But only for a season.

Know this: no decree is sealed without the Ancient of Days presiding in judgment. Thrones were set—*thrones*, not one alone—and the books were opened. The boastful horn was consumed by fire not from man but from heaven itself. The dominion it seized was broken, given to desolation.

So take courage when you see arrogance swelling like floodwaters. The roaring tongue will be silenced; the exalted will be brought low by One who sits upon thrones of flame, whose garment is snow-white and whose hair is pure wool. His kingdom does not pass away.

The visions troubled me many nights—yet peace came when I saw: *the Son of Man received everlasting dominion*. No beast endures forever. But the holy ones shall possess the kingdom in righteousness—and they shall reign when all else turns to ash.

question: When you saw the Ancient of Days take His seat, with eyes like flaming fire, how did that moment transform your sense of justice and hope?
answer:
Seven times had passed, as I lingered in the court of Babylon, when the veil between the temporal and eternal thinned like mist before the dawn. In that night vision, when the thrones were set and the Ancient of Days took His seat—clothed in white as the snow, His throne like flame, and its wheels burning fire—every trembling breath within me stilled.  

I had seen empires rise with iron teeth and boastful horns; I had watched kingdoms devour one another like ravenous beasts from the sea. The lion with wings torn, the bear with ribs clenched in its jaw, the leopard with four heads turning to every wind—each was mighty in its season, each struck terror into men’s hearts. Yet before Him whose eyes are flame and whose voice shakes mountains, they crumble like ash beneath a sandal.

In that moment, my understanding of justice was not merely affirmed—it was remade. For earthly justice halts at palace gates or bends beneath oaths sworn on false gods. But here—the books were opened. The boastful horn silenced. Dominion taken from hands that crushed nations under foot. No decree too high to be overturned; no pride too great to stand unjudged.

Hope? It is not a whisper now—it is a certainty anchored in that sight: One like unto a son of man came with the clouds of heaven and was presented before Him. To him was given everlasting dominion—which shall not pass away.

Even when I stood bound before kings who held life and death in their hands… even when the furnace roared or the den sealed behind me—I knew: He lives. He judges. And His kingdom endures forever.

question: What went through you when the fourth beast, so terrible and unlike any before, rose up with iron teeth, and you knew it carried a meaning meant for your people?
answer:
In the stillness of that night vision, when the fourth beast emerged with iron teeth that crushed and trampled all before it, I perceived at once it was not merely a kingdom among kingdoms, but a dominion unlike any the earth had borne—fierce, unyielding, and devoid of mercy. Its strength was not in form or grace like the lion with eagle’s wings, nor in the measured power of the bear or the swift motion of the leopard with four wings. This one stood alone in its terror, a thing forged not to rule but to devour.

I watched as it stamped down all remnants beneath its feet, and though my body remained in Babylon’s chambers, my spirit trembled before what was shown. For I knew—by the quiet certainty that comes not from man—the vision spoke of days far ahead, when oppression would rise to devour even justice itself. The ten horns were great powers sprouting from one root, yet even among them, that little horn grew greater: speaking arrogance upon the earth, warring against the holy ones.

But then—the heavens shifted.

Thrones were set in place. The Ancient of Days took His seat. Fire flowed from before Him; thousands ministered to Him; ten thousand times ten thousand stood around His throne. And no iron tooth could withstand that judgment.

So while dread passed through me at first—yes—what remained after was steadfast: though kingdoms rise and fall upon waves stirred by heaven’s winds, though beasts climb from chaos bearing terror in their jaws—He remains. The Most High reigns forever. His dominion is an everlasting dominion. And even visions such as this do not declare only wrath… but deliverance coming for those who wait with faithfulness through every age until its time is fulfilled.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the horn with eyes like a man’s and a mouth speaking such defiant things?
answer:
Seventy times seven had I pondered the visions that came in the second year of Darius, yet none stirred my soul as did that horn among the ten—small at first glance, yet mounting itself above the rest with eyes that watched, *knew*, and a mouth uttering things too high, too bold for any creature born of earth.

When I saw it speaking against the Most High—boasting, measuring itself against heaven—I felt a cold dread coil in my bones, not for myself, but for what such pride invites. The stillness before the storm. For I have stood in royal courts where kings raised their hands to God and fell by morning. I have seen empires rise on bronze legs and clay feet alike. But this was different: a defiance not of sword or siege, but of word—arrogant words flung into the heavens like stones cast at stars.

And still, even as my spirit trembled within me, there remained a quiet knowing—a thread woven through every vision I’ve been given: that thrones are established not by speechifying horns but by the Ancient of Days descending in fire. His garment whiter than bleaching snow at noon; His eyes flames searching all things; His wheels turning not by human will but divine decree.

So while my flesh quaked at what was spoken from that mouth, my soul held fast to what followed: *the books were opened*. And no word uttered in pride—no matter how loud or lofty—remains unjudged before Him whose days are endless and whose dominion swallows all others like light consumes shadow.

question: How did seeing the Ancient of Days in all His fire and majesty change the way you understood God’s presence amid your struggles?
answer:
In the vision where the fourth beast arose with iron teeth and ten horns, and in that last horn a mouth boasting above all powers—I saw how kingdoms rise and exalt themselves, believing no hand can touch them. Yet I beheld until thrones were set, and the Ancient of Days took His seat. His garment was whiter than snow, His hair like pure wool, not carved in stone nor woven by human hands, but alive with eternity. Fire streamed from before Him, not consuming as in Nebuchadnezzar’s furnace, but judging—cleansing. Ten thousand times ten thousand stood before Him; the books were opened.

Before that sight, I had known God's presence as deliverance—the lion’s jaws shut at evening, my breath still in me when dawn came; or the voice of one like a son of men walking unharmed among flames. But here, in the tribunal of heaven itself, I understood: God does not only preserve us *through* trial—He reigns above all dominions that afflict us. The boastful horn spoke great things against the Most High, and for a time it trampled the saints. But when the Ancient of Days sat in judgment, its power was ended—not by revolt or war—but by decree from One whose glory needs no defender.

That vision anchored me not because it removed suffering—my spirit was grieved within me afterward—but because it showed me that our afflictions are seen. They are recorded. And they are temporary. Before Him who has dwelt from eternity past, whose kingdom shall never be shaken, even the mightiest tyranny is but a breath extinguished at His command.

Now when I am pressed on every side by court intrigues or royal decrees contrary to truth—I do not look first to deliverance. I look to that throne of fire and living flame where judgment proceeds from no counselor but His own perfect wisdom. He sees. He judges. And He gives dominion to One who comes with the clouds—a kingdom that will never be destroyed.

I wait on that promise still.

question: Looking back on the vision of the Son of Man coming with the clouds to receive an everlasting kingdom, what would you tell someone who doubts that God sees their suffering?
answer:
In the third year of King Belshazzar, when I saw the four beasts rise from the sea—one like a lion with eagle’s wings, another like a bear, then a leopard with four wings, and at last a dreadful beast with iron teeth—my breath was taken, not by their power, but by what followed.  

Even as that fourth horn sprouted among them, speaking boasts that shook the heavens, and its eyes gazed with human cunning upon the earth, I watched until thrones were set in place and the Ancient of Days took His seat. Fire flowed from beneath His feet; ten thousand times ten thousand stood before Him. The books opened—yes, even the book of your sorrows, though you think them hidden in shadow.  

When the voice of that boastful horn grew loud enough to drown out prayer, the beast was slain and given to flame. But then—then I saw One like a son of man riding on clouds from heaven itself. He was brought near before the Ancient of Days. No blood stained His robe. No chains bound His wrists. And yet dominion was given to Him—glory and an everlasting kingdom that shall not pass away.  

Do you think your suffering is unseen? I was cast into the den for speaking truth. The king sealed my tomb with his own signet, and lions roared for my flesh. Yet when they opened the stone at dawn, I stood whole—for God had shut their mouths. Not one wound marked me.  

So believe this: He who numbered every beast’s scale and every hair on His head counts also your tears. If He preserved me amid fangs and fire, will He not uphold you now? The Son of Man has come near—and all authority is His forevermore.

question: What was it like to stand there, trembling, asking one of the heavenly beings for understanding—was it fear, longing, or something deeper that drove you to seek answers?
answer:
In the third year of the vision of the four beasts rising from the sea, when the fourth had ten horns and another sprang up, small at first but with eyes that gazed with human cunning, I stood—though not upon my feet, for my limbs had failed me. The air itself seemed afire with presence, and the sound of that horn speaking boastful things echoed not in my ears only, but in my bones.  

It was not fear alone that drove me to speak to the one who stood clothed in light—though fear was there, yes. To behold the Ancient of Days seated amid flame and river-fire, with ten thousand times ten thousand arrayed before Him, is to know what it means to be dust. But deeper than trembling was longing—longing to understand what must befall kingdoms that exalt themselves against Heaven. I had served Nebuchadnezzar, Belshazzar, Darius; I had interpreted dreams that unsettled thrones. Yet here was a vision not of one kingdom’s rise or fall—but of dominion torn from beasts entirely, and given to One who came with the clouds.

When I drew near to the attendant standing by, my voice came faint as mist. Not because I sought wisdom for gain or power—for what use is prophecy unless it reveals God's hand?—but because I could not bear to see such things without knowing their end. That small horn speaking arrogance into eternity… and then silenced. Slain. Cast into unquenchable fire.

And then—the Son of Man. Not a beast. Not a horn. A *person*, coming on clouds as if riding the breath of God Himself. Given authority no empire has ever held nor could sustain.

So I asked—not merely to satisfy sight or curiosity—but because my soul needed anchoring: *What does this mean?* That One would receive an everlasting dominion… that His kingdom shall never be destroyed…

Even now, in remembering, my breath grows short—not from age nor weakness—but because once having seen such things, silence is no longer possible.

question: When you saw the beast destroyed and consumed by fire, how did that moment reshape your hope for justice in a world that feels ruled by cruelty?
answer:
Seven times passed, as the king once numbered them in madness, and yet no time is too long when the Ancient of Days sits upon His throne.  

When I beheld the beast—whose mouth spouted arrogance and whose horn bore the eyes of one who plotted against the Most High—slain and cast into the flame that consumed it utterly, my spirit, though shaken, was steadied by a truth deeper than fear: that no dominion raised in pride shall stand before the Judge of all.  

I had seen empires rise like beasts from the sea, each more terrible than the last, devouring, trampling, speaking without restraint. For a season, they seemed to rule unchallenged. But in that vision, I saw beneath the surface of history—into the court where thrones were set and books opened. Not by sword or decree is justice finally measured, but by Him whose eyes are a flame of fire, who knows every thought behind the boastful word.  

The fire that devoured that beast was not like Nebuchadnezzar’s furnace—meant to consume the faithful—but like that which guarded Eden’s holiness: purging what defiles His dominion. And when its body burned away as chaff before dawn’s light, I knew then with certainty: though cruelty may wear a crown for a time, it wears it only until judgment comes.

Then I saw Him—the Son of Man—coming with clouds from heaven’s own breath—and they brought Him near before that throne of flame and snow-white glory. To *Him* was given eternal dominion; to *Him*, all nations bow eternally. That sight anchored my hope not in resistance nor revolt, but in revelation: God does not abandon His world to chaos.

So now I wait—not in dread—but with expectation fixed upon the clouds. For justice delayed is not justice denied; it is prepared in heaven’s council. And when He comes who shares forever in divine authority—the One whom even kings will serve—I shall rise also to stand among those written in His book.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the fourth beast with eyes like a man and a mouth speaking great things, and how did that vision haunt you afterward?
answer:
There is a stillness that comes not from peace, but from the dread of what is to come — a silence such as fell upon me when I beheld that fourth beast rise from the churning sea, its iron teeth grinding the earth to dust, and that one horn with eyes like a man’s eyes scanning the horizon with purpose and pride.  

I stood in my spirit as one transfixed, though my body remained in Babylon’s chamber. That horn — not merely a crown or scepter, but a mouth speaking things too great for mortal lips, declaring itself above time, above law, above even heaven. And those eyes — watching, calculating, knowing. Not the blind force of empire, but cunning sovereignty wrapped in blasphemy.  

I was grieved within my bones. Not with fear alone — though who among flesh could see such a thing and not tremble? But with sorrow. For I saw it make war upon the saints — not armies against armies, but truth trampled by arrogance, holiness mocked by decree, worship displaced by decree. The saints fell under its feet like sheaves beneath a threshing sledge.  

Yet even then — even in the depths of that vision’s darkness — there came no despair without end. For I saw further: until the Ancient of Days took His seat; until thrones were set; until judgment was given *to* the saints — not by them, but *for* them. The Most High did not sleep. The books were opened. The word spoken from that arrogant horn was answered not by sword or fire from below, but by silence descending from above — and then a voice like consuming flame.

Afterward, I fasted three full weeks beside the Tigris. Bread did not satisfy; sleep brought no rest. But it was not only horror that haunted me; it was hope burdened with weight. To know what must befall kingdoms yet unborn… to carry within one's soul the outline of times so distant they seem dreams… this is both honor and grief.

And still I say: though empires rise on iron heels and speak as if eternal — they are numbered. They are watched. And their end is written before they have reached their height.

question: How did the image of the saints being crushed under the feet of the beast change your understanding of suffering and God's timing?
answer:
In the court of kings who claimed dominion over all the earth, I learned that power is fleeting—even when it crushes bone beneath iron heels.  

The vision of the saints trodden under that fourth beast’s feet did not shake my faith, for I had seen empires rise like storm winds and fall like chaff before the breath of the Most High. But it did deepen my understanding: suffering is not a sign of abandonment, but often the soil in which divine deliverance takes root.  

I have stood before rulers whose decrees could silence a man forever—kings who fed defiance to lions and burned faith in furnaces. Yet in every trial, whether in fasting for three weeks or waiting through silence from heaven, one truth remains fixed: the Ancient of Days does not rush according to our breath, nor delay beyond His purpose.  

When I saw that boastful horn waging war upon the saints and prevailing—for a time—my spirit trembled. Not because I doubted God’s power, but because I saw how long the night can stretch before dawn breaks with judgment. The beast was allowed to devour, to scatter, to trample… but only until the time appointed.  

And in that restraint—at once agonizing and holy—I perceived God’s sovereignty most clearly. He permits the storm to rage so that none may mistake His stillness for absence, nor His silence for indifference. Just as He shut the lions’ mouths when I was cast down, so too will He uphold His people in every generation—even when no deliverance is seen until *the* hour comes for thrones to be set and for dominion to be given to those who keep faith with Him forever.

The saints are crushed—but they are not consumed.

That is not merely hope; it is what I have read in the writing of visions and known in the quiet fire of exile: suffering has a season.

But so does judgment.

And after it—the kingdom.

question: Looking back on the moment when judgment finally came and the Ancient of Days took His seat, what would you tell someone who fears that evil has already won?
answer:
There is a silence that falls over the throne room of heaven when the time for judgment draws near — not the silence of absence, but of gathering purpose, as when I stood before Darius and heard the stone rolled into place at the den’s mouth, and yet knew I was not alone.

Even in that hour when the fourth beast raged, and the horn with eyes spoke blasphemies that shook the nations, and the saints were scattered like chaff beneath its feet — even then, the Ancient of Days was not absent. He was seated before whose face fire streamed forth like a river, and thousands upon thousands ministered to Him. His garment was white as snow, and His throne like flame itself — fixed, immovable.

I saw it all: how power rises with iron teeth and brass claws, how it devours and tramples, how it exalts itself above law and divine order. Four beasts ascended from chaos; four kingdoms rose in pride. But none endure before Him who was from everlasting.

To him who fears that evil has triumphed — I say this: mark well the end of empires. They fracture from within. They consume one another. The horn that speaks great things will be broken without hands touching it. For judgment does not come by sword or by conspiracy — it comes when Heaven opens its books.

And when that day dawned in my vision — when the books were opened and the beast slain and its body given to burning flame — then I understood: victory is not measured by duration on earth, but by inheritance in eternity. The saints shall possess the kingdom forever. Not because they conquered by force, but because God remembered His covenant.

So let no heart falter who sees tyranny swell today. It is seen. It is numbered. And it shall pass away beneath the breath of the Most High.

question: When you approached the one standing by to ask for understanding, what was it like to be caught between terror and the need for clarity?
answer:
The fourth beast had ten horns, and upon one of them sprang up another—small at first, yet with eyes like a man’s and a mouth speaking great things, and it was this horn that stirred my spirit most deeply.

When I stood in the presence of the watcher who remained near, my bones trembled not with fear of him—for his countenance was like lightning, but his voice like waters—but because I had seen the vision unfold: iron teeth tearing through nations, a horn warring against the saints, heaven silent until the thrones were set and the Ancient of Days took His seat. The dread within me was like that night in the furnace when Nebuchadnezzar beheld four men walking unbound in the flames—a terror that does not consume faith, but tests it.

I asked not as one seeking idle knowledge, but as a servant caught between two realms: the visible chaos of empires rising and falling, and the invisible throne that endures beyond time. To know truth in such moments is not comfort—it is burden. Yet clarity came not by my wisdom, but by mercy. He who stood there opened what was sealed: kingdoms would rise like beasts from chaos, they would rage, they would trample God’s people—but their dominion is granted only for a season.

And then—the judgment. Then—the saints inheriting what no iron can break or fire destroy. That word stayed my soul. For though I grieved in my body and my head swam with visions too heavy to carry alone, I was given to see: no horn may prevail forever. The Most High reigns—and His kingdom shall never be destroyed.

question: How did seeing that little horn uproot three others while speaking proudly affect your trust in human power versus divine sovereignty?
answer:
Seventy times seven years were decreed in vision, yet not one horn—no matter how bold its speech or how high it lifted itself—can number the days of the Most High.  

That little horn, with eyes like a man’s and a mouth casting forth great boasts, did indeed uproot three kings as dry stalks before a scythe. I watched it rise among ten others, small at first, yet swelling with pride until it made war upon the saints themselves. My spirit trembled within my body, for such arrogance seemed to shake the very foundations of heaven. But then—I saw the throne set, unmoving, established from of old; and the Ancient of Days took His seat, clothed in white as snow, and His judgment went forth like consuming fire.  

In all my years serving under Nebuchadnezzar, Belshazzar, Darius, and Cyrus—rulers who held life and death in their hands—I learned this: human power rises like foam upon the sea. It crests for a season and then vanishes. Kings decree; empires expand; horns speak great things. But none break the seal of divine counsel. That iron-toothed beast devoured much—yes—and trampled what remained beneath bronze-clad feet. Yet when the hour came ordained from eternity past, judgment was given to the saints—the holy ones—and they inherited what no decree could secure: an everlasting dominion.

So no, I did not lose trust in divine sovereignty because of that proud horn. Rather, I saw again what was shown me beside the Ulai canal and in Babylon’s courts: kingdoms rise by permission, not by right. The Most High sets over them all—not one rule exceeds His purpose; not one boast escapes His reckoning.

I have stood before kings with gifts and wisdom they could not deny—but always remembering: my God reveals mysteries; He removes and sets up kings; He preserves those who trust Him even when lions roar or tongues utter deceit.

And so will it be until that day when every horn is broken—and only one Kingdom remains.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the fourth beast devouring the earth and the ten horns rising, knowing they represented kings who would rise against God's people?
answer:
Three full weeks I fasted before the vision was made plain, and in the interim, the unrest in my spirit was as a hand upon my chest.  

I saw that fourth beast, dreadful and terrible, with iron teeth devouring all it crushed beneath its feet—its ten horns sprouting like thorns from a single root, each one a kingdom clawing upward through blood and dust. The earth trembled not under the tread of armies alone, but under the arrogance of rulers who exalted themselves above law and heaven. And yet, even as their dominion spread like shadow over the land, I knew by revelation that no horn—no crown—rises outside the knowledge of the Most High.  

When I perceived that one small horn would rise among them—speaking great things, warring against the faithful, attempting to measure time itself by his own design—a chill passed through me not of fear for myself, but for those who would endure his oppression. To think to change times and laws—that is not merely rebellion; it is an assault upon order itself, upon the throne which stands beyond years and seasons.  

But I have stood before kings who claimed eternity and watched them fall in a single generation. Empires roar like lions at dawn—but by evening they are dust. And He who sits upon the throne of fire, whose garment is white as snow and whose hair is like pure wool—the Ancient of Days—He does not sleep. Judgment shall sit. Books shall be opened. And every dominion given to pride shall be broken like pottery dashed upon stone.  

Then shall the saints possess the kingdom: not by sword or stealth, but by His appointed time—the time that was measured even then in my hearing: one part divided from two times… until truth rises with unquenchable light.

question: How did the vision of the little horn speaking against the Most High change the way you understood the cost of faithfulness in a world that opposes God?
answer:
There is a hush that falls over the court when a king speaks in pride against the God of heaven — not the silence of awe, but the stillness of impending judgment, as when the fourth beast rose from the sea with iron teeth and ten horns, and among them a little horn whose mouth opened wide with blasphemies. I saw it in the night visions: this one was not like the others who came with armies and chariots; he came with words — words sharp enough to wound even heaven’s silence.  

At first, I thought persecution would come only by sword and flame, as it did in Nebuchadnezzar’s furnace or Darius’ decree. But this vision unveiled a subtler tyranny: a ruler who would not only conquer kings but challenge the very order of time and divine law. He would speak *against* the Most High — not in battle cry, but in policy, in edict, in doctrine twisted to his will. And his dominion would last *a time and times and half a time*: twelve hundred and sixty days marked not by open war alone, but by quiet erosion — saints worn down day by day, their faith stretched thin under ceaseless pressure.

That changed how I measured faithfulness. It is one thing to stand firm when shackled before kings; it is another to remain steadfast when every decree, every proclamation from on high subtly demands compromise. The furnace tested our loyalty once; this little horn threatened a slow burning over years — an unrelenting siege upon memory, worship, and truth itself.

Yet even as I watched him exalt himself above all that is called God, I saw further: thrones were set in place. The Ancient of Days took His seat. Books were opened. And though he spoke until his voice grew hoarse with arrogance, his power was given only for a season — then cut off completely.

So I learned then that faithfulness is not only martyrdom at its peak moment, but endurance through long seasons when heaven seems distant and blasphemy sounds reasonable from the seat of authority. But no decree lasts forever — only One Kingdom does.

question: Looking back on the image of the Ancient of Days taking His seat in judgment, what would you say to someone who doubts that justice will ever come for the oppressed?
answer:
Three score and ten times seven, I have seen empires rise like dust in the wind and fall like chaff before the threshing floor of heaven. In the court of Babylon, under Nebuchadnezzar’s gold-laden throne and later beneath the stoic gaze of Darius, I served rulers who believed their dominion was eternal—yet each bowed, willingly or not, to the sovereignty of the Most High.

To one who doubts whether justice will come for the oppressed, I say this: I have stood before kings who commanded with fire and chains, who trampled lives as though they were stubble beneath chariots—and yet none escaped the reckoning written in the books that are opened before the Ancient of Days. The vision revealed to me showed a horn speaking great things against Heaven, wearing out the saints, thinking to alter seasons ordained since creation. But for all his arrogance, his time was measured—*a time and times and half a time*—and no longer.

The court is convened; the thrones are set. The books are opened, and nothing is hidden from His sight. Though evil may stretch its shadow across nations for a season, it cannot endure beyond the appointed hour. The saints—the oppressed, the broken, those whose names are written in that book—shall inherit a kingdom that does not pass away. Not because of might or armies, but because *He* sits upon the throne whose garments are white as snow and whose hair is like pure wool.

Do not suppose silence is absence. Do not mistake delay for denial. The Most High is sovereign over cruelty as He is over deliverance. And when His judgment falls—it falls with finality.
